title: The 2026 Value Creators Rankings: Strong Markets, Hard Strategic Choices
gist: BCG's 2026 Value Creators rankings show a decade-long leadership rotation away from software/IT toward asset-heavy sectors (mining, oil and gas, aerospace/defense, construction, banking), driven by AI value accruing to physical infrastructure, geopolitics redirecting capital to tangible assets, and banks benefiting from higher rates—while record valuation 'expectation premiums' raise the bar for future returns.

insights.claim: In BCG's 2026 Value Creators ranking, asset-heavy industries—mining, oil and gas, aerospace and defense, construction, and banking—have taken the top value-creation positions previously held by technology-driven sectors for most of the past decade.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Investors benchmarking to last decade's tech-led leadership should reweight toward asset-heavy cyclicals, which now dominate five-year TSR rankings.
insights.quote: Asset-heavy industries such as mining, oil and gas, aerospace and defense, construction, and banking now hold top positions that technology-driven sectors occupied for much of the past decade.
insights.locator: Global stock markets continue to deliver strong

insights.claim: Software and IT services fell from 4th place in BCG's 2025 Value Creators ranking to 31st in the 2026 ranking, a 27-place drop, moving from among the top five industries to the bottom five.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: A 27-place fall in one year signals a genuine re-rating, not noise—investors should scrutinize software multiples for further compression rather than assume mean reversion back to tech leadership.
insights.quote: The decline of software and IT services—a 27-place drop—is particularly striking: the sector was among the top five industries last year but now sits in the bottom five.
insights.locator: Beneath this stability, however, the composition

insights.claim: Within technology, performance has diverged sharply: hardware and electrical-components makers are outperforming, while software, IT services, financial infrastructure providers, and medtech are underperforming.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Treat 'tech' as two distinct trades—AI-adjacent hardware/components (favored) versus software/IT services and other tech-enabled verticals (disfavored)—rather than one sector.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, performance within technology has diverged

themes.theme: AI Value Accrual to Physical Infrastructure

insights.claim: BCG argues that among public companies, AI-related value is currently accruing to the physical infrastructure layer—chips, data centers, power infrastructure, and physical connectivity—rather than to software, because the current AI deployment phase is capital-intensive.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Favor AI capex beneficiaries (semiconductors, data-center hardware, power/electrical-equipment suppliers) over AI application/software plays until software monetization proves out.
insights.quote: Among public companies, AI value is accruing to the physical infrastructure layer.
insights.locator: Several forces are driving this rotation

insights.claim: BCG states that the AI-driven revenue uplift many investors expected for software companies has not yet materialized at scale, and that AI itself is beginning to disrupt parts of the software value chain.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: This is a direct counter to the consensus 'AI will supercharge SaaS revenue' narrative—investors should stress-test software names for AI-driven disintermediation risk rather than assume AI is a pure tailwind for incumbents.
insights.quote: The AI-driven revenue uplift many investors expected has yet to materialize at scale, while AI itself is beginning to disrupt parts of the software value chain.
insights.locator: Software companies, by contrast, face a more uncertain

themes.theme: Geopolitics Redirecting Capital to Tangible Assets

insights.claim: BCG links accelerating defense spending across Europe and Asia, rising energy-security priorities, sharply higher oil and raw materials prices, and increased infrastructure investment (from supply-chain fragmentation) to why aerospace and defense, oil and gas, mining, metals, and construction now make up five of the top six performing sectors in the 2026 rankings.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Geopolitically-driven capex (defense, energy security, reshoring infrastructure) is a durable, multi-year tailwind for hard-asset sectors, not a one-off spike—supports overweighting defense, mining, and materials names.
insights.quote: These trends directly favor aerospace and defense, oil and gas, mining, metals, and construction, now five of the top six performing sectors.
insights.locator: Geopolitics and trade policy are redirecting capital

insights.claim: BCG notes that a sustained period of elevated interest rates has improved net interest margins for banks, driving rising bank stock valuations, and that investors appear to be betting these rate tailwinds will outweigh private-credit risk concerns even as inflation stays above pre-COVID lows.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Bank outperformance is a rates-regime trade contingent on inflation staying structurally higher; a return to pre-COVID low-rate conditions or a private-credit blowup would remove the key support for bank valuations.
insights.quote: With inflation not returning to its low pre-COVID levels, investors appear to expect these tailwinds to outweigh concerns about private credit risks.
insights.locator: Banks have benefited from the higher-rate environment

insights.claim: BCG observes that regionally, the US continues to dominate in scale and mega-cap concentration, Asia remains overrepresented among top-performing companies, and Europe lags despite signs of stabilization.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Regional allocation should tilt toward Asia for top-performer density and the US for mega-cap scale, while treating European equities as a laggard bet on stabilization rather than a value-creation leader.
insights.quote: While the US continues to dominate in scale and mega-cap concentration, Asia remains overrepresented among top-performing companies, and Europe lags despite signs of stabilization.

insights.claim: Global equity markets have delivered average returns of roughly 12% annually since 2020, only modestly above the 10-to-15-year historical average, despite the post-COVID stall and the April 2025 correction tied to shifting US trade policy.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: A ~12% annualized return baseline is the bar top-quartile companies and sectors must clear; use this figure as the market median reference point in the article's own top-quartile-outperformance analysis.
insights.quote: Global markets have delivered average returns of approximately 12% annually since 2020.
insights.locator: Markets Are Resilient, But the Landscape Is Changing

insights.claim: An unprecedented 11 companies exceeded $1 trillion in market capitalization by the end of 2025, reflecting extreme concentration at the top of global equity markets.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Mega-cap concentration at this scale increases index-level fragility to any single trillion-dollar name's derating; watch breadth metrics alongside headline index returns.
insights.quote: An unprecedented 11 companies exceeded $1 trillion in market capitalization at the end of 2025.
insights.locator: Markets Are Resilient, But the Landscape Is Changing

insights.claim: In 32 of the 35 industries BCG studied, top-quartile companies outperformed the 12% market median TSR—including companies in sectors that ranked near the bottom of the overall industry rankings.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Stock selection within a lagging sector (e.g., software) can still generate market-beating returns—don't write off an entire sector; hunt for company-specific outperformers even in out-of-favor industries.
insights.quote: In all but three of the 35 industries studied, top-quartile companies outperformed the 12% market median—including in industries that ranked near the bottom of the overall rankings.

insights.claim: BCG's framework holds that sector rotation shapes the terrain but does not determine company-level outcomes: strategy, capital allocation, operational execution, and adaptability are the real differentiators of shareholder returns.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: When screening for investments, weight company-specific capital-allocation discipline and execution quality as much as or more than sector tailwinds.
insights.quote: Although the macro environment shapes the terrain, it does not pick the winners.
insights.locator: In other words, although the macro environment shapes

themes.theme: Record Valuation Premiums and the Expectations Gap

insights.claim: For US non-financial companies, the 'expectation premium'—the gap between current market value and underlying fundamental value—has reached its highest level since 1926, exceeding even the peak of the dot-com bubble in 2000.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: This is a stark valuation warning: US equities are pricing in more future growth than at any point in nearly a century, including the dot-com peak—a key risk metric for gauging downside if growth expectations disappoint.
insights.quote: For US non-financial companies, this premium has reached its highest level since 1926, exceeding even the peak of the dot-com bubble in 2000.

insights.claim: BCG frames the central corporate challenge as balancing near-term performance expectations against longer-term reshaping of business portfolios and capital reallocation toward emerging value sources, a process it calls complex and difficult to execute.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Evaluate management teams on their explicit near-term vs. long-term capital allocation trade-off communication, not just quarterly beats.
insights.locator: Companies, therefore, face a critical balancing act

insights.claim: BCG distinguishes near-term value creation levers (valuation multiple uplift, large capital returns like share buybacks) from longer-term outperformance drivers (profitable revenue growth, disciplined capital allocation, valuations reflecting genuine future upside).
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Distinguish companies that are 'renting' TSR via buybacks/multiple expansion from those building durable growth—the latter are better long-horizon holdings.
insights.locator: Near-term performance can be boosted by an uplift

themes.theme: Strategic Playbook: Act Like an Activist on Yourself

insights.claim: BCG advises that successful leadership teams should approach capital allocation and portfolio decisions with the urgency of activist investors, using tools including significant cost reduction, major changes to capital allocation priorities, and portfolio reshaping via divestitures.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Investors can use this as a screen: management teams proactively divesting and reallocating capital (self-activism) are more likely to sustain TSR than passive incumbents waiting to be targeted by external activists.
insights.quote: Successful leaders will approach these decisions with the urgency of activist investors, leveraging a tool kit that includes significant cost reduction, major changes to capital allocation priorities, and portfolio reshaping (including through divestitures).
insights.locator: Control What You Can—and Bring Investors Along

insights.claim: BCG argues companies currently benefiting from sector tailwinds (e.g., defense, mining, banks) should use the current strength to deliver strong through-cycle performance rather than overspend in response to newfound prosperity, while companies under pressure should prioritize securing their financial foundation and freeing up capital for investment.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Watch for capital discipline signals in currently-favored cyclical sectors (defense, mining, banking)—overspending during the current boom is a red flag for future TSR reversal.
insights.quote: For some companies, this will require using the current tailwinds to deliver strong through-cycle performance rather than overspend today in response to their newfound prosperity.
insights.locator: For some companies, this will require using

insights.claim: BCG contends that companies making the right strategic choices but failing to bring investors along will not see those choices reflected in valuation or TSR, making investor communication and expectation management a source of compounding advantage.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Good strategy alone doesn't guarantee re-rating; assess management's investor-communication track record as a distinct factor from strategic quality when forecasting TSR.
insights.quote: Companies that make the right strategic choices but fail to bring investors along will not see those choices reflected in their valuations and TSR.
insights.locator: In a market defined by extremely high expectations

insights.claim: BCG suggests some companies will need to actively reshape their investor base toward long-term holders whose horizon matches company strategy, rather than short-term holders focused on quarterly results, framing investor-base quality itself as a strategic asset.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/hady-farag
insights.so_what: Track shifts in a company's shareholder register (growth of long-only/patient capital vs. short-term/hedge fund holders) as a leading indicator of strategic execution runway.
insights.quote: In a market this expectation-laden, the quality of a company's investor base is itself a strategic asset.
insights.locator: Some companies will also need to actively reshape
